sentences.text: Yes.
My name at birth is Braindel Helfer, H-E-L-F-E-R.
B-R-A-I-N-D-E-L.
My day of birth is August 20, 1921.
I am just turned, a few days ago, 74.
I was born in Chrzanow, Poland.
C-H-R-Z-A-N-O-W.
Well, the city is not far away from the well known Auschwitz.
Well, we had a family of five, three brothers and a sister.
I survived with my sister.
My father was a tailor.
Well, I have very good recollection.
Being the baby of the family, I was treated a little special.
And a nice home.
I would say my father struggling with to make a living, after he came home from being five years in Siberia from the first World War.

And I tried to help by father with thread the needles, cleaning up the garments, delivering-- and occasionally I got a little tip, which was very welcome-- and to rip old garments to make all over again if the material was good.
But as a whole, my mother, housewife, religious observant.
And good recollections.
Yes, yes.
Well, the religious and the upbring, I appreciate my mother's effort to send me to a Jewish school by [NON-ENGLISH].
And until today, I can read and write Yiddish, which the language was at that time, and Polish, which I learned in elementary school.
Went through, then later on in the years, I wanted to go to a school away from home, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but finances did not allow.
In the early teens.
I would say 13, 15, yeah.
My sister went too, yes.
Yes, and the brothers helping out the family to sustain.
Fairly close, yes, fairly.
I remember I used to play volleyball at school, and little activities.
I liked to go in to watch ice skating.
I was dreaming to go.
And I watched [NON-ENGLISH], which I was satisfied.
And winter time was the snow, which until today I liked.
I like the snow, but I wouldn't like to live in a country where the snow is.
I loved the mountains.
And this was the general activities.
A little in the woods we had the close by to pick up blueberries, and little, I think, mushrooms too.
So it was a little, it was a little variety.
Nothing can compare to think of today.
Yes, we had cousins, and my aunts that lived not far.
And they used to come.
And they used to bring the children to stay for the summer, which we were very fortunate to, to be able to treat them.
And the same times, we gained too, because if we went to a farm to have some fresh milk, fresh, fresh cheeses, sour, sour yogurt.
So it was real nice.
That was a real vacation time.
And holidays, were very observant too.
This I don't remember having them.
I don't think whether I even know about this word bar mitzvah.
If they went, maybe they had only in the temple.
But I don't recall any of the bar mitzvah.
Yes, we even had in our close by on our back yard.
There was one part of the house, of the next door house, that it was like small steeple.
Like they call it like a little house where the congregants got together and they were praying.
But the main synagogue was we had to walk.
And everything was close by, was in a walking distance.
The Jewish community was very nice, especially Shabbat.
I remember I used to go Saturday with my mother to visit someone.

And yes, I remember too that when it was, I think, Purim, [NON-ENGLISH], the offering, used to go with a little plate to put on a cube of chocolate on a piece of cake, and an apple if we could afford.
But I remember very well the cube of chocolate.
Not a block of chocolate, how you call it, but a cube, a small piece of chocolate.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] That was candies.
No, candies I don't remember, but I remember very vividly the chocolate.
[SIGHS] I really don't remember exactly.
Being still in a young age, I wanted-- I didn't know the existence-- oh, yes, I, I remember I went visiting to my aunt, so I saw another town, which opened my eyes a little.
But there was no time to dream.
No, no, I was too young, too.
Well, the last-- just before the war, I think, there was the family, we got the little business, where to iron the clothes.
They call it a [NON-ENGLISH].
It was like a heavy box and a wax inside.
It used to be operated by hand.
And later on, it was operated by a motor, so the electric one was much easier.
And I to put, like roll up the, the [BACKGROUND NOISE] clothes, sheets, and shirts, and towels, and make it like a roll, and to roll it under this, which operated the [NON-ENGLISH].
And it came out with a shine, just like ironing.
You didn't have to iron.
No, I was only one who operated it.
It was physical work.
And sometime my sister helped me too.
My mother occasionally too, yes, but she was practically the housewife.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And my father was busy.
The brothers did not help, no.
It was like a source of the income going into the war.
There was people come in and exchange food for labor.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And I did some too, some work, fixing nylons.
Not nylons, but silk stockings.
When they have the runs, there is a special needle to pick it up and to, not to sew them away, but to fix them.
And that was a very tedious work, and I did it.
Anti-Semitism, especially, personally myself, I don't know.
But I know that we didn't go close-- oh yes, in school I remember when we had to, we had to stand up when the Christians said their prayers.
We had our hands together, and we stood up.
But I don't remember exactly whether I was-- in my years, no.
I used to, I used to hear the word Jew, especially on Passover, that when they say that we used to put in the blood into the matzos.
I-- no.
No, I wouldn't say.
As a matter of fact, I had this a friend that was not Jewish, a personal friend.
And I think after the war, I, I contacted her.
She sent me a picture, her picture, to Paris even, yeah.
But recently, I didn't hear from her, no.
Well, when the, [SIGHS] when the war broke out in 1939, in September or August, yeah, we were-- we knew that there was the persecution of Jews.
And we had to go to work for the German.
Nothing too serious yet.
I-- I personally was not alert or aware of it yet, until later on when it was-- when they caught my brother to-- well, it was at night, knocking at the door, coming in--
Well, we used to, they call it [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means that they knock at the door and come in, and we all, of course, afraid.
Sometimes they took someone, sometimes they didn't.
It depends who's luck was it.
So my brother, my oldest brother was the first one to go to the camps, to the working camp.
Well, he must have been that time maybe in the late-- just a minute. '33-- he must have been in the early 20s that time.
Yeah.
Herschel.
That was the oldest brother.
He went and we knew that he's in a working camp, and nothing much.
We expected something.
But there was talking between us, and exchanging news.
And my father was very, very, delicate.
He says, oh-- he was very sorry that he lived through a time that we have to be fearful and not to be free.
After my brother Herschel was taken away, I think there was the middle brother, [?
Hidel. ?] He was taken away too.
I think he was too, maybe a few years different.
Maybe a year or two different the brothers were.
So he was taken, I think, from the working from the shop where we use to work for the, for the military.
So over there, they used to take and pick up some workers too.
So from him, we didn't have any sign.
And I didn't know until today were any sign.
But from the older brother, I know that there was an accident where he used to work, and he died.
He got killed, it's an accident.
He fell down.
And he was buried at the camp over there.
So how was it in your home after the two brothers left?
What happened then?
Well, myself, and my sister, and my father, we used to-- we went to the shop to work for the German.
Sometimes it was day time, sometimes it was night, night shifts.
It was the uniforms.
I used to though-- I used to do hand work.
My sister, I think, and my father did operating in a machine.
And I used to work, hand work.
I remember I used to join the collar.
It was, it was like finishing from the garment.
[SIGHS] I personally have very little recollection about the-- as I got very little is that how to survive.
And the war is we try to hear something about the war.
Somebody maybe heard something from a radio, from hiding, about the war, at the end of the war.
I think I remember I had one teacher in elementary school, and he was not Jewish.
And he was taken to Dachau.
And I think about a few months later, or maybe a year later, he, he came back.
But he came back very thin, very not to recognize him.
So we sort of like got the message that it's not, it's not a picnic.
My third brother died of TB in 1933, just quite before the war, which was a very big tragedy.
My mother was very much mourning after this brother.
Yes, he was the younger one.
He must have been in his early-- in his late teens, maybe.
Yes, was young.
Yes, yes.
But he was-- I think he was taking, now as I gather, he must have taking as a political prisoner, not as a Jew.
That must have been.
But nothing else.
No other recollection I have.
But this remained in my mind.
Then came 1943.
There was the general cleaning up from Jews in our hometown.
They came.
They knocked at the door, and everybody to go out.
And one of the SS men said, take the keys, because you're going to come back.
So myself--
That was exactly February 17 or 18, 1943.
So my sister, and myself, and parents, we went to the main, main place in the town, like sort of gathered everyone, all the Jews.
And there they separated my parents and myself and my sister.
My sister and myself, they sent us to a working camp, to Neusalz.
[SIGHS] I remember the keys, that they said, take the keys, you're going to go home.
So whether they said for me or my sister to take the keys, or the parents.
And that was all.
Nothing.
We didn't have anything.
No, no.
[SIGHS] Now as I recollect, I don't, I don't remember exactly.
It was sort of like a mixed emotions.
It was excitement.
Knowing-- we knew about Auschwitz.
We didn't know exactly, or maybe I didn't know exactly what's going on over there.
It was 1943.
From '39 to '43, it was already four years, not quite four years.
So it's-- no, I personally don't remember exactly what.
But later on, we didn't hear anything from 'em.
And we got some messages what's going on in Auschwitz.
After I left the parents?
I was with my sister.
So that was-- I had here and she had me.
So we both together stick together, and we were able to be together all the time.
No, it was us--
It was us.
It was us.
We went only to younger generation.
The older one were separated.
And we were sent to, to another place, like a gathering.
And from there, they sent away in groups to places where they needed workers.
Yeah.
We were together all the time.
Well, we were sent, we were sent in trains.
I think in trains, yeah.
No, I don't remember, no.
I just remember that we were sent to, to a camp, and that was Neusalz.
Maybe February, maybe beginning of March.
Yes, other ones that came from our home town, and maybe from other towns too that they gathered together.
[SIGHS] No, no.
I cannot remember, no.
No, no.
We just looked for the next day and that says it.
Well, that train was not as bad, because we said we were sent to a camp to work.
We were lucky that we didn't go into Auschwitz.
It was in Neusalz.
It was with some our home town friends.
Neusalz--
--in Germany.
It was a mill, [NON-ENGLISH], I think a cotton mill.
[NON-ENGLISH].
The name was [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, we got acquainted.
We went to work.
And we looked for the day to get our meals.
We were hungry.
And I have very little recollection from the camps.
A matter of fact, I have some sort survivors here that were together with us.
And they ask me, Betty, don't you remember that, and that we were in the same room, and I don't.
Well, those were livable conditions.
Yes, Neusalz was livable conditions.
Yes, a matter of fact, I personally have got recollections from Neusalz, because I had a very good kind of work, affordable work, where I could meet some prisoner-- French prisoners of war.
And by that type of work, this prisoner could put in some Red Cross goodies into my basket, what I had.
And I could share.
And I remember now, and until today, I have a habit to part.
When I am with my sister, or with anyone, I share, I cut in half.
Sharing.
Oh, the French the French prisoner of war, no.
No.
But there was over there one German.
And I'm sure that he has seen it and he ignored it.
So that was a help.
So it was-- I was lucky.
And then I had a woman too, who, every day, she put in one place like a container with soup for me.
And one German woman gave me a toothbrush, which I could bring it in the camp and exchange for some soup.
If I had another toothbrush, I would.
So as my recollection, as I remember Neusalz, the good things, that I could survive over there, with my sister.
Though my sister didn't have this kind of work.
She had no opportunities to help herself.
So both together, we shared and we survived Neusalz.
Yes, she worked next to me.
And I supplied her with, with the able, that she would be able to work the waltzes.
That it goes like a wooden waltz that I brought from the French prisoner to supply her.
So it was a good connection.
I was lucky with that.
Of course, this women, if she would, if she would be caught, she would be maybe punished.
But she tried to help us.
She was very compassionate.
I wish if I would see her now, if I would know her name.
And I am grateful to her, yeah.
The daily routine was getting up and to count us, [INAUDIBLE] on the place, and then to march to the work, because the factory was close by.
The barracks were close by to the factory.
And to go to rush to be on time, not to be late, and to come back.
Once I worked the night shift, which until today, I remember very, very well.
It's the worst time is our 1, 2, and 3 o'clock in the morning.
That was the worst hour.
You fell asleep.
And daytime, we could visit a little, and clean up the barracks to keep it clean, to do some house chores, because there were inspections in the barracks, duties, certain duties.
And if one could have a duty in the kitchen, that was a reward.
You could have another slice of bread, or another potato, or anything.
It was-- so my recollection about Neusalz was not bad.
We had bunk beds, up and down.
And I had my sister.
I think we slept with my sister together, yes.
So we kept warm at night--
--cuddling.
I recall very little at the barracks.
I remember-- well, I remember the work, and that helped me to survive, and to be helpful to my sister too.
And hoping that one day we will, we will get together and, and have our parents.
But later on, it went too far.
It was, it was a little less and less hope.
Hope?
Yes.
We never lost hope.
One day, I remember there was one French soldier who was a little, little guy.
And he was Jewish.
And he told us once when Rosh Hashanah comes up.
He was really influential, and he knew.
So he tried to help.
So it was really very, very helpful, and optimistic too.
